chunks: Maya’s mind whites out, a static scream of panic that deafens her to the thud of her father’s footsteps crossing the bedroom floor, the squeak of the closet door opening, the rustle of fabric as he moves toward the dresser. She stands frozen on the landing, Ethan rigid beside her, both of them listening to the sound of their own destruction being inventoried above. Then her mother screams. Not a loud scream, but a small, wounded sound like a rabbit caught in a trap, and Maya’s eyes snap open to see her mother’s finger pointing not at the bedroom, but at the floor by the entryway where Ethan’s backpack lies open, its contents spilled across the tile—his school laptop, crumpled papers, and a glossy photograph that must have fallen from one of the pockets when he dropped his bag earlier in his haste to get to the couch, to her, to the filming. It is a printout of the thumbnail from their video. The one showing Maya’s face mid-orgasm, Ethan’s cock visible at the edge of frame. Ethan must have kept it, a souvenir, a trophy, and now it lies face-up on the hardwood like a dropped hand grenade. "Lord God," her mother whispers, swaying, her hand clamping over her mouth. Her father barrels back out of the bedroom, drawn by the commotion, and sees the photograph. He stops, his face draining from purple to ash-gray, the Bible falling from his hand to thud against the floor. He picks up the photo with two fingers, holding it at arm’s length as if it might contaminate him, his eyes flicking from the image to Maya’s face, to the marks on her neck, to Ethan’s horrified expression, connecting the threads with the terrible efficiency of a man who has spent twenty years studying human sin. "You," he says to Ethan, his voice barely a breath. "With your own sister."  Maya’s legs buckle. She sinks to the floor, her knees hitting the tile with a crack that she doesn’t feel, the world narrowing to the sound of her own heartbeat and the certain knowledge that they are ruined.

But then Ethan moves, stepping forward with a sudden, desperate energy, placing himself between the photograph and their parents, his hands raised in surrender. "It’s not real," he blurts out, the lie tumbling out in a rush of words. "It’s edited. It’s... it’s a fake. We found it online, we were looking at it because... because we were trying to figure out if it was actually us, if someone was pretending to be us, because Jason showed me this site and there were people using our names, our pictures, and we were scared—"  "Shut up," their father roars, the sound shaking the windows, but Maya seizes the thread, her survival instinct kicking in through the haze of terror.
